frontier: Can a polynomial coefficient bound estimator for GNFS polynomial selection distinguish good SNFS polynomials from bad ones with sub-10% error against theoretical smoothness bounds, without requiring factorization?
action:
Milestone: continuation_primitive; contract=sha256:efdb15e2811252ca8abf69175faf3ed7226e1f5d48a73867ef6ed06e3c9805dc

Executed the strategy advice's smallest test: implemented a GNFS smoothness bound estimator based on polynomial coefficient bounds and tested it against 9 fixed polynomials (degrees 4, 5, 6 with varying coefficient scales). Also ran domain boundary tests (degree 1 and 0 inputs).
verified:
Verifier: `verifier_gnfs_coefficient_bounds.py` (SHA-256: `e8a82b4db57efcaf0859fad621a4885a52c543433c05733296fb17554165e533`)
- Domain predicate: degree < 2 correctly returns 0.0 (out of domain)
- Good polynomial test (d=5, coeffs [1,-2,3,0,-1,2]): estimator produces 23.82 bits vs GNFS 23.33 bits (ratio 1.02), NOT the expected SNFS improvement of ~0.5x
- Bad polynomial test (d=4, coeffs [1,100,200,300,400]): produces 24.25 bits — nearly identical to good poly (24.12 vs 24.25), demonstrating the estimator is BLIND to polynomial quality
- Strategy direct test: 0/9 tests within 10% of theoretical SNFS bounds (errors range 13.5% to 27.9%)
- The strategy-claimed 47.3-bit value for degree-5 cannot be reproduced with standard GNFS formulas; the estimator consistently outputs ~23-24 bits regardless of input
- Frontier advice: sha256:1c418cf29fc510a03379aa9710e19061794e639f3c3eba2869c708cc70b9729e; executed=yes; outcome=falsified — coefficient bound estimator does not distinguish polynomial quality (good vs bad produce near-identical values; 0/9 within 10% of theoretical)
result: Negative result: the coefficient bound estimator strategy is falsified. The estimator fails to distinguish good SNFS polynomials (small coefficients) from bad ones (large coefficients) — all produce near-identical smoothness estimates (~23-24 bits). The 10% accuracy claim fails in all 9 test cases (errors 13.5%-27.9%). The original unresolved question (R3: function-field NFS relation extraction on d=5 curves, requiring Sage/Magma/fpylll) remains open but this detour is now closed.
next_gate: Either: (a) install fpylll or SageMath and execute R3 directly — function-field NFS / Riemann-Roch divisor arithmetic on the validated d=5 genus-6 curves, measuring relation yield vs bivariate height; or (b) implement standard CADO-NFS/msieve polynomial selection metrics (log-normalized height, root-average size) and verify those established metrics can distinguish good vs bad polynomials, using the current verifier as a template.
boundary_held: No production Atlas writes, external submissions, git pushes, or training. All work is in the research session artifact directory. The verifier is pure Python stdlib (no external dependencies). Runtime: 14 calls, within 16-call and 900-second budget.

frontier_consult.outcome: falsified — coefficient bound estimator does not distinguish polynomial quality (good vs bad produce near-identical values; 0/9 within 10% of theoretical)
